author: @gechgr1850
text:
Pretty interesting in that it has relative simple construction.
However, if you keep reducing the size and offset of the arrangement of the panels, you would end up with what is essentially a helical swept-blade wind turbine, which I suspect becomes more efficient relative to its construction.

author: @rickymcguire9493
text: Has anyone tried also combining a turbine like this with some photovoltaic cells to make up some of the outer surface area. So the constant turning would give them constant exposure to supplement the energy output of your model?  Just a thought. Cells can be made very thin and light . Think about it.

author: @colleenforrest7936
text:
Just to keep things in perspective... A family of 5 died in Louisiana due to Hurricane Laura because they brought their propane generator indoors. Having a generator that could be safely operated indoors durring a storm isn't useless DYI.  

Being a domed shape, I'm curious how well this generator would hold under Hurricane force winds that would shread traditional wind generators.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is a good point

author: @MrFmiller
text: As a concept it’s intriguing. I like that it might take advantage of the vertical as well as horizontal orientation of the air flow. It would be interesting to find out how it might compare to conventional blades. I suspect the density of the blades might increase the relative torque. It would require professional testing to ascertain actual efficiency.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it would - and I can't do that - I don't have the experience or the equipment

author: @kenrowe167
text: We are all praying for wind to see it in action. I thought the last design was cool - this is even cooler! The centre sphere is the optimal shape from a fluid dynamics point of view, I think. Though it will be subject to the Magnus Effect which produces a force normal to both the wind direction and the axis of rotation. The same force that causes a football to swerve when you bend it like Beckham. That's how Flettner rotors work. The support structure needs to be strong enough to withstand it.

author: @NorthernKitty
text: Oh, wow!!  That is gorgeous!!!  And that went from "concept" to "completion" lightning fast!!  I'm looking at the sphere in the center and realizing the shape could be used to help airflow.  It would be interesting to try different sculpted shapes and see how it effects efficiency.  I'm also just now realizing that with how easy and cheap you can make those "blades", you don't really have to worry about excessive winds.  Just make a bunch to keep on-hand - if/when the wind shreds your blades, just replace them!  😋  Unless you live somewhere like Iceland, that shouldn't be too frequently.  This is by far one of my favorites builds!!  😍

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome lol - I love it too - but yes I do work quickly when I have a mind to lol - I like the idea of different shapes - fancy giving it a go?

author: @Route72Trucks
text:
Really an amazing device Robert! Something to be quite proud of.

I have often wondered if one could generate a usable amount of electricity to charge a battery bank you carry in your car. Say a cylindrical squirrel cage lying on its side mounted to the bottom of the vehicle with a shroud that only allows the air to interact with the squirrel cage at the perfect angle. Or possibly mounted to the roof or trunk. Utilizing areas of drag that already exist in the vehicles design. How much could one generate during a 45 minute commute?  

I love your videos brother! Our world is a better place for your genius. Thank you

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: if you attach it to a car mate it will burn more fuel because of the wind resistance it will create

author: @theresahartman7465
text: I’m wondering whether putting a few of these on a rack on the back of a vehicle could produce power while driving, or when there’s not enough sunlight for solar power?

replies.author: @johnmcfadden9336
replies.text:
Theresa Hartman I think with the right design one could gain power from the air motion over a moving vehicle but I don’t think these would suit in this application as the would produce extra drag on the vehicle and reduce the mpg 
If the power produced was used to generate hydrogen this improves the engines combustion significantly in older models 
😃

replies.author: @bj2048
replies.text: @johnmcfadden9336 i keep thinking about using the waste heat from a car engine to generate some power. Something like a thermoelectric generator but more efficient. Using waste heat wouldn't add additional load to the engine.

replies.author: @theresahartman7465
replies.text: What about using it when stopped, keeping it in an enclosed space while mobile?

replies.author: @johnmcfadden9336
replies.text: Theresa Hartman definitely worth it on camper vans and other vehicles too and some how store the energy

replies.author: @johnmcfadden9336
replies.text:
Bill Jones hi I don’t know why the waste heat from the exhaust isn’t used to power absorption cycle heat pump for air cooling and even refrigeration I know these are not as efficient as normal compression cycle units but the heat is there for the taking  
I don’t know if it’s feasible to run a turbo just to generate power for hydrogen use 
To be honest I’m no expert on mechanics or anything come to that but I do know we are we far to wasteful 
We’ve been conditioned and encouraged to be since childhood and are now held to blame for the destruction of our beautiful planet so ear mark for elimination to save the world 😃

author: @jamest.5001
text:
I have alot of trees around my house, the reason I haven't put up a turbine, is it would have to be about 50' high to get good wind,   maybe if I made a large one of these, about 4' x 4' squares, what ever that comes to. Just a guess, anaybe 8-10' dia, maybe this could work on my roof? On about a 10'-12' pole, being the wind is coming through the trees, if I could get about 150 watts on a day with decent wind, it may be worth putting up, but for me to put money into a turbine, it would need to do a minimum of 400-500 watts, and about 2kw in a good storm!   And speaking of turbines, what's your opinion of a 6 blade turbine, with 3 short and 3 long skinny blades ? The short being about 8' diameter, and the long about 12' dia. .
I'm thinking it will start at low speeds, and keep going in higher winds,, what's your $0.02??

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I did the original version in the trees if you remember and the idea here is to try and capture buffeting winds - so it wold be well worth the experiment I would think - especially if you build it yourself - which is very do-able. I don't know about a six blade design mate - I haven't really tested it so I can't really say anything about it - sorry

author: @MartinPHellwig
text: Hmm can you try it out flat wall mounted when you have a bit of wind?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @Draakdarkmaster6
text: i know you said you dont want to change it at all, but its occurs to me that with very little changes you could stack these vertically to increase output without having to build more horizontally, building vertically allows you to basically have a wind tower that generates power per individual unit, instead of the standard wind turbine model that generates per tower.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: go for it mate - chances are I won't I am afraid

author: @sofa-lofa4241
text:
Great series Rob,
It's been a great illustration of how to take a simple idea and take it through a rough and ready design and build phase,
You don't need CNC's, 3D printers and lasers (actually, yeah, it needs more lasers!) 

Many designs that look so complex that only a computer could design them can just be condensed down to a good idea and a few simple rules and tools to design and build them, this build has been a great example of that, (another one I can think of would be Canterbury Cathedral... Same principles! 📏📐⚒️) 

The fact that it looks like the evil love child of a tie fighter and the death star is just a bonus!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - I agree with the love child thing especially mate lol - I often think people get too tied up with 3d printing when a few tools and you can do more, cheaper and quicker - but I guess that's the way some folks think

author: @ChristieNel
text: What about a reverse Voith-Schneider?  Would that work?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I had to look that up mate - which should tell you - I just don't know - but I like the idea

replies.author: @ChristieNel
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering My thinking was that Voith-Schneider can change the thrust vector instantaneously, so maybe one can use it in reverse, using a small "rudder" to adjust its airfoils to match the wind direction.  Not all that clear on the details, but maybe there's potential.

Yay! I have been waiting for your video to come out. I do every day to see what awesome thing you have come up with next. I love the wind gen. It looks really awesome! I am currently working on a pendulum style generator but I saw some limbs in my yard moving this AM as they often do with light wind and I want to come up with a generator from it. 
Anyway, I will stop rambling and get back to work. Keep them coming!!
